text.title: STAR Method Core Framework
text.summary: STAR component definitions, interview flow, follow-up question types, and complete vs. incomplete response patterns.

rule.statement: Every STAR question must use past-tense behavioral framing: "Tell me about a time..." Never use hypothetical framing ("How would you..."). Hypothetical answers have near-zero predictive validity.
rule.rationale: McDaniel et al. (1994) meta-analysis: structured behavioral interviews are ~2x more predictive than unstructured. The behavioral framing is what produces the predictive signal; hypotheticals do not.

list.item: Situation: the context or background — "Tell me about a time when..."
Task: the specific challenge or responsibility — "What was your role/responsibility?"
Action: what the candidate specifically did — "What did YOU do?" (not the team)
Result: the outcome and what they learned — "What was the result? Can you quantify it?"

example.code:
1. ASK: "Tell me about a time when you had to deal with a difficult stakeholder."
2. LISTEN for Situation: - Specific context (project, team, timeframe) - Who was involved
3. PROBE for Task if missing: "What was your specific responsibility?" "What were you trying to accomplish?"
4. DIG INTO Action: "Walk me through exactly what you did." "What was YOUR role, not the team's?"
5. CLOSE on Result: "What was the outcome?" "What metrics can you share?" "What would you do differently?"

example.note: Complete STAR: all four components present, personal contribution clear, result quantified.
example.code: "When X happened (S), I was responsible for Y (T), so I did A, B, C (A), resulting in 30% improvement in Z (R)."

example.note: Missing Action: no personal contribution visible.
example.code: "The project was struggling, so we fixed it together as a team."
"I led a team through a challenging period and we worked really hard."
"I would approach this by first understanding all stakeholders' needs, then building consensus around the solution."
